title: Can More and Better Jobs Help Save Brazil's Forests? Introducing Erysichthon's Curse
gist: A new World Bank paper coins 'Erysichthon's Curse' — a development trap where economies fail to create enough good jobs while depleting natural capital — and shows in Brazil that coupling agricultural productivity gains with manufacturing and services productivity growth reduces Amazon deforestation more than agricultural productivity gains alone, which can backfire via a land-clearing Jevons effect.

insights.claim: Erysichthon's Curse, a concept introduced by Marek Hanusch and co-author Joaquim Bento de Souza Ferreira Filho, names the development trap in which economies struggle to create enough more and better jobs while simultaneously losing natural capital, drawing on the Greek myth of a king cursed with insatiable hunger who consumed everything that sustained him.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
entities: Joaquim Bento de Souza Ferreira Filho
insights.so_what: A new named framework for evaluating whether a country's growth model is depleting the natural capital it depends on — usable as a screening lens for EM growth/ESG theses.
insights.locator: In a recent paper, my co-author Joaquim Bento de Souza Ferreira Filho

insights.claim: The world needs around 800 million additional jobs over the next decade, particularly for young people, while simultaneously approaching environmental tipping points that threaten the natural systems prosperity depends on.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Frames the jobs crisis and the environmental crisis as the same macro-level constraint, not separate policy tracks.
insights.locator: The world needs around 800 million additional jobs

insights.claim: The cure to Erysichthon's Curse is not less growth but a different pattern of growth — one that creates more and better jobs while safeguarding natural capital.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Positions the policy prescription as growth-composition reform rather than degrowth, relevant to how development finance should be allocated across sectors.
insights.locator: Our paper shows that the answer is not less growth

insights.claim: Brazil's export basket remains dominated by commodities, reflecting relatively weak productivity growth in manufacturing and modern services, even as almost 90 percent of its population lives in urban areas and many Brazilians work in low-productivity, informal jobs.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Identifies a structural mismatch in Brazil — urbanized workforce, but underdeveloped manufacturing/services productivity — as the underlying vulnerability the paper's model addresses.
insights.locator: Brazil is one of the world's great agricultural success stories

insights.claim: Brazil holds 60 percent of the Amazon rainforest, and although Amazon deforestation has fallen substantially in recent years, around 5,100 km² of rainforest were still lost in 2025.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A current, dated deforestation figure (5,100 km² in 2025) for tracking Brazil's environmental trajectory against its jobs/growth policy shifts.
insights.locator: Progress has been made. Amazon deforestation has fallen substantially

insights.claim: The standing Amazon generates ecosystem services worth an estimated US$317 billion every year, regulating rainfall across South America, storing vast amounts of carbon, and harboring unparalleled biodiversity, with losses extending well beyond Brazil to affect climate stability, food security, and biodiversity globally.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A quantified valuation ($317B/year) for Amazon ecosystem services, useful as a baseline for natural-capital or carbon-market pricing discussions.
insights.locator: The standing Amazon generates ecosystem services worth an estimated

insights.claim: In agricultural frontier regions such as the Amazon, higher agricultural productivity can increase profitability and encourage farmers to expand production into forests — a land-use version of the Jevons effect where efficiency gains increase, rather than decrease, resource consumption.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A contrarian caution for climate-smart agriculture investment theses: raising farm productivity in frontier zones can accelerate deforestation rather than curb it.
insights.locator: In agricultural frontier regions such as the Amazon, higher productivity

insights.claim: When agricultural productivity gains are spread more evenly across regions — including mature farming areas with little pressure on forests, rather than concentrated in frontier zones — deforestation falls.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Suggests agricultural investment/extension programs should deliberately prioritize mature farming regions over frontier zones to avoid inadvertently accelerating deforestation.
insights.locator: When productivity gains are spread more evenly across agriculture

insights.claim: Coupling agricultural productivity growth with higher productivity in manufacturing and other traded sectors reduces Brazilian deforestation even further than agricultural productivity gains alone, because most Brazilians now depend on manufacturing and services for income, and productivity growth there creates jobs while reducing the incentive to expand agriculture into forests.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: The core policy finding: non-agricultural productivity investment (manufacturing, services) is an indirect but more effective deforestation lever than agricultural productivity investment alone.
insights.locator: More surprisingly, coupling agricultural productivity with higher productivity

insights.claim: Brazilian total factor productivity and deforestation in Brazil's Amazonian states move closely in tandem (inversely), based on simulation results in the underlying paper.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Empirical evidence linking macro productivity trends directly to deforestation trends supports treating TFP growth as an environmental policy lever, not just an economic one.
insights.locator: Figure 1 shows how closely Brazilian total factor productivity

insights.claim: In Brazil, urban productivity investments — better transport, digital infrastructure, reliable electricity, skills, and a stronger business environment — both create jobs and reduce deforestation, and because Brazil's electricity system is already largely renewable, they also help reduce greenhouse-gas emissions.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Identifies a genuine triple-win investment category (jobs, deforestation, emissions) for Brazil-focused infrastructure capital, at the intersection of the World Bank's Jobs and Livable Planet agendas.
insights.locator: In Brazil, investments in urban productivity

insights.claim: The authors pose as an open question for future research whether Erysichthon's Curse could also help explain deforestation, ecosystem loss, and tipping points in other parts of the world beyond Brazil.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Signals the framework may be extended to other frontier-deforestation economies (e.g. Indonesia, DRC) — worth watching for follow-on research applying the same jobs-versus-natural-capital lens.
